data.title: Broadcom Is Maintained at Buy by Benchmark
India bonds rise on short covering; government commits to meeting fiscal target
data.description:
Indian government bonds advanced on Friday as traders unwound short positions on easing fiscal concerns and an assurance from government officials that the budget gap target for the year will be met.

The benchmark 10-year bond yield (IN063335G=CC) settled at 6.4651%, compared with Thursday's close of 6.4934%.

Bond yields move inversely to prices.

Finance Minister Nirmala Sitharaman said in an interview with Network18 on Friday that the government will meet its budget targets, including its fiscal deficit goal.

"Can say with confidence, capital expenditure of the government will not come down from the budgeted level. Same with fiscal deficit. This is the last point of my fiscal glide path, I will adhere to it," Sitharaman said.

The federal government's fiscal deficit is targeted at 4.4% of the gross domestic product in the current financial year.

Late on Wednesday, India lowered goods and services tax rates on hundreds of items to boost consumption. Ahead of the formal announcement, bond markets had feared a material fiscal slippage due to the cuts, but the government pegged the revenue loss at a manageable 480 billion rupees ($5.46 billion).

The 10-year bond yield has risen around 27 basis points in July and August due to an uncertain outlook for rate cuts and government finances.

Rising bond yields are making borrowing unaffordable for federal and state governments, Sitharaman said.

"Hopefully, there will be a follow-up action from the government to address this, which is positive for bonds," Debendra Kumar Dash, senior vice president of treasury at AU Small Finance Bank, said after the Finance Minister's comments.

Discussions are ongoing between the central bank and market participants on the second-half borrowing programme, amid rising optimism that the government will reduce the supply of long-dated papers.

RATES

India’s overnight index swaps ended lower on Friday, as traders continued receiving across the curve on easing fiscal concerns.

The one-year OIS rate (INR1YMIBROIS=CC) was down 2 bps at 5.4850%, while the two-year OIS rate (INR2YMIBROIS=CC) fell nearly 2 bps to 5.4525%.

The five-year OIS rate INR5YMIBROIS=CC fell 3 bps to 5.7150%.

data.title: This Customer Engagement Software Stock Is Up 19%. Here's Why. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Mackenzie Tatananni

Braze stock surged in premarket trading Friday after the customer engagement platform posted a solid second quarter and boosted its financial guidance.

Adjusted earnings of 15 cents a share came in above analysts' calls for three cents a share, according to FactSet. Revenue surged 28% from the prior year to $180.1 million, topping the $171.6 million Wall Street had forecast.

The software maker also more than doubled its forecast for its full-year profit. Braze sees adjusted earnings in the range of 41 cents to 42 cents a share, compared with its previous outlook of 15 cents to 18 cents and above the 17 cents analysts were looking for. The company expects revenue between $717 million and $720 million, up from a prior range of $702 million to $706 million. Wall Street had anticipated $704.5 million.

Mizuho analysts noted Friday that Braze delivered a "strong beat and raise" in the latest quarter. "Operating margin of 3.4% exceeded consensus of 0.7% and should alleviate investor concerns on margin expansion," the team said. Mizuho reiterated an Outperform rating on Braze shares and raised its price target slightly to $45 from $40.

The stock soared 19% to $32.87 on Friday. Futures tracking the benchmark S&P 500 and tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite were up 0.2% and 0.5%, respectively.

Write to Mackenzie Tatananni at mackenzie.tatananni@barrons.com

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1153 GMT - Societe Generale rates strategists remain cautious on French goverment bonds, and recommend waiting for more clarity before contemplating buying bonds on dips, they say in a note. French Prime Minister Francois Bayrou faces a confidence vote on Sept. 8, while likely countrywide industrial action and protests loom on Sept. 10. Fitch Ratings reviews France's ratings on Sept. 12. The 10-year French OAT yield falls 2.4 basis points to last trade at 3.478%, while the 10-year French OAT-German Bund yield spread is 77 basis points, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

1150 GMT - U.K. inflation remains higher than that of the U.S. and the Eurozone, and appears to be heading to 4.0%, Moneyfarm's Richard Flax says in a note. That's higher than the 2% Bank of England target. The U.K. also seems to be running a larger budget deficit compared to some of it's developed market counterparts, Flax says. These factors are likely why the yields on U.K. sovereign bonds are higher, he says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

1130 GMT - U.K. assets including sterling, government bonds and stocks are little moved after U.K. Deputy Prime Minister Angela Rayner said she would resign after failing to pay enough tax on a property purchase. The move was widely anticipated with an independent ethics advisor looking into whether she broke ministerial standard rules. The government is already facing a challenging outlook with rising debt levels ahead of the autumn budget on Nov. 26. Fiscal sustainability concerns sent long-dated bond yields surging earlier in the week, denting sterling. The currency rises 0.3% to $1.3475, little changed after the news. Ten-year and 30-year gilt yields are steady at 4.700% and 5.551%. respectively, Tradeweb data show. The FTSE 100 rises 0.3% to 9243 points. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

1123 GMT - As political turmoil continues in France, French government bonds are expected to continue to underperform, Societe Generale's rates strategists say in a note. They prefer other eurozone government bonds, with Italian BTPs their top pick. BTPs offer the best carry and room for further spread tightening, the strategists say. Elsewhere in the eurozone, Belgium's fundamentals are weak, but foreign demand and lower volatility make the country's bonds a good risk-adjusted play, they add. Finland and Austria are facing worsening debt dynamics, while the Netherlands stands out for its fiscal stability despite political noise, they say. "[Dutch government bonds] are resilient, but we're cautious on the long end due to fading pension fund demand." (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

1109 GMT - The steepening of the long end of the Japanese government bond curve has reached extreme levels, which aren't fundamentally justified, BlueBay Asset Management's Mark Dowding says in a note. A 167 basis point spread between 10- and 30-year JGB yields is unheard of in developed bond market valuations, the CIO says. "At this point we think that the Japanese Ministry of Finance would be well advised to announce that it will only re-issue long dated bonds should the spread to 10-year JGBs normalize back to 75bp, or less," Dowding says. Elevated 30-year yields also create a negative impression of Japan's vulnerability to fiscal sustainability. The 30-year JGB yield last traded at 3.229%, up 0.5 basis points, according to LSEG. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

1058 GMT - The steepening theme across global bond curves persisted at the start of this week, with investor enthusiasm for long-dated maturities continuing to diminish, BlueBay Asset Management's Mark Dowding says in a note. Largely speaking, demand for long-dated bonds has historically come from pension funds seeking to match the tenor of their assets to long-dated liabilities, the CIO says. "However, as defined benefit schemes have closed and those remaining are already duration matched, this means there is structurally less demand for long-dated debt," he says. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

1045 GMT - The recent rise in ultralong government bond yields reflects investors' concern about persistently high government budget deficits and correspondingly swelling government debt, says LBBW's Elmar Voelker in a note. This not only leads to an oversupply of government bonds, but fears that inflation could rise again in the longer term are being stoked, too, the senior fixed income analyst says. "In the USA in particular, the issue of 'debt inflation' is currently acute in view of the Trump administration's attempts to gain control of the central bank and use this to reduce the government's interest costs," he says. In the eurozone, smoldering fears of a debt crisis in France are once again coming to the surface, the analyst says. (emese.bartha@swsj.com)

1023 GMT - Sterling faces renewed falls ahead of the U.K. autumn budget on Nov. 26, Ebury strategist Matthew Ryan says in a note. Sterling has recovered modestly from losses sustained earlier this week following a selloff in long-dated U.K. government bonds amid fiscal concerns. Bonds have bounced back in the U.K. and globally but risks remain elevated, Ryan says. "It would be foolhardy of us to bet that the worst of the gilt selloff is behind us." Markets will punish the government if it fails to deliver a budget that guarantees fiscal sustainability, he says. Sterling rises 0.3% to $1.3479, having reached a four-week low of $1.3330 on Wednesday, LSEG data show. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

1013 GMT - U.S. Treasury yields continue to fall ahead of the closely watched August nonfarm payrolls data at 1230 GMT. Analysts' consensus in the Wall Street Journal's poll is for an addition of 75,000 jobs, with the data likely to cement Federal Reserve rate cut expectations. "With respect to markets and the Fed, a September cut is all but a lock in the wake of [Fed Chair Jerome] Powell's Jackson Hole speech which was once again defined by a shift in the balance of risks to the labor side of the mandate," Natixis IM Solutions' portfolio strategist Garrett Melson says in a note. The 10-year Treasury yield falls 1.5 basis points to 4.160%, while the 30-year yield trades down 1.8bps to 4.854%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

1008 GMT - The cost of insuring euro credit against default falls due to improved sentiment on prospects of weak U.S. jobs data causing the Federal Reserve to cut interest rates. The U.S. non-farm payrolls data due to be released at 1230 GMT is expected to show weakness in the labor market, raising the possibility of several interest rate cuts by the Fed for the remainder of 2025. The job openings data and ADP jobs data released this week came in weaker-than-expected, and it will be no surprise if the non-farm payrolls data is also weak, Rostro's Joshua Mahony says in a note. The iTraxx Europe Crossover index, which tracks euro junk bond credit default swaps, declines 2 basis points to last trade at 261bps, S&P Global Market Intelligence data show. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

1007 GMT - A strong surprise in U.S. nonfarm payrolls data could fuel volatility in gold prices, DHF Capital's Bas Kooijman says in a commentary. The precious metal is trading near record levels as investors stay cautious ahead of the U.S. jobs report that could shape sentiment and monetary-policy expectations, he notes. Recent U.S. data, including weaker private payrolls, have reinforced expectations of a dovish Federal Reserve, with traders nearly certain of a 25bp reduction at the central bank's September meeting, he says. Investors are likely betting on up to three cuts this year, he adds. Spot gold rises 0.2% to around $3,552/oz. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

1000 GMT - Better-than-expected U.K. retail sales data Friday should have limited implications for sterling, Monex Europe analyst say in a note. Retail sales increased 0.6% in July compared with the previous month, above the 0.5% forecast by economists in a WSJ survey. The data aren't indicative of a resurgence in consumer demand and the Office for National Statistics has showed inaccuracies in its monthly calculations, the analysts say. Sterling rises 0.3% to $1.3478. The euro trades flat at 0.8668 pounds. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

data.title: Tesla Board Proposes Musk Pay Package Worth as Much as $1 Trillion Over Decade — WSJ
data.description:
By Gareth Vipers

Tesla's board is asking investors to approve a new pay package for Chief Executive Elon Musk that could be worth as much as $1 trillion over the next decade.

The proposed arrangements could see Musk, already the world's wealthiest individual, awarded various installments of shares dependent on Tesla hitting a series of milestones, according to a financial filing published Friday.

More to follow as news develops.

data.title: Celsius Holdings Is Maintained at Buy by Needham
Samsara Stock Is Rising on Earnings. The IoT Company Won Some Big Customers. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Nate Wolf

Shares of Samsara were rising sharply in premarket trading Friday after the "internet of things" company beat earnings and revenue expectations for its fiscal second quarter and notched several major customer wins.

The company, which provides technology to connect physical operations like construction and transportation, posted adjusted earnings of 12 cents a share, surpassing analysts' estimates of 7 cents. Revenue totaled $391.5 million, up 30% from the prior year and above Wall Street's call for $372.2 million.

Samsara shares were climbing 12% to $40 in premarket trading Friday. The stock closed Thursday down 18% for the year.

The company added a quarterly record of 17 customers with $1 million or more in annual recurring revenue, or ARR. Overall ARR totaled $1.6 billion, up 30% from last year.

Samsara boosted its fiscal-year guidance following the second-quarter results. The company expects revenue growth of 26% from the prior year, up from a previous forecast of 24%, with adjusted earnings of 45 cents to 47 cents a share, compared to a previous range of 39 cents to 41 cents.

Write to Nate Wolf at nate.wolf@barrons.com

data.title: European stocks rise, yields ease, focus on US jobs data
data.description:
Key points:
  • European stocks rise, after S&P record high
  • Traders wait for U.S. jobs data, focus on rate cut expectations
  • Oil prices in third day of declines

European equities rose on Friday while long-dated bond yields eased, as expectations for U.S. rate cuts helped markets overcome concerns about fiscal deficits in various countries.

The S&P 500 hit a new all-time high on Thursday after weekly jobs data showed more jobless claims than expected. Asian stocks tracked Wall Street higher overnight.

The MSCI World Equity Index was up 0.3% on the day EURONEXT:IACWI at 1127 GMT while Europe's STOXX 600 was up 0.3% , set to end the week slightly higher overall after recovering from a dip earlier in the week.

The FTSE 100 was up 0.3% CURRENCYCOM:UK100 and France's CAC 40 was up 0.1% .

Wall Street looked set to continue the gains with S&P 500 futures up 0.2% and Nasdaq futures 0.5% higher on the day.

Markets are all but certain of a quarter-point cut at the conclusion of the Fed's two-day rate-setting meeting on September 17, according to LSEG data.

Traders will be looking to the monthly U.S. jobs report due later in the session to confirm their expectations. A weaker labour market boosts stocks because it raises expectations that the Federal Reserve will cut rates.

"We have already seen yesterday the sign that perhaps there will be a weakening in jobs, paving the way for a done deal in September," said Francesco Sandrini, head of multi-asset strategies at Amundi.

Today's numbers "can confirm to some extent an easing stance of the Federal Reserve," Sandrini added.

Fed Chair Jerome Powell had already reinforced rate cut speculation with an unexpectedly dovish speech at last month's Fed symposium in Jackson Hole.

"Unless it's an absolutely stellar payrolls print, it's hard to see too much that's going to change the market away from locking in a September cut," said Ken Crompton, head of rates strategy at National Australia Bank.

"Beyond that, the terminal rate and how you get there, that's arguably still up for grabs."

Market sentiment has recovered in recent days after global stocks fell earlier this week and long-date bond yields in Europe hit their highest in years, as investors became concerned about the state of various countries' finances, particularly Britain and France.

Yields eased on Friday, with France's 30-year yield at 4.3873%, down from a peak of 4.523% on Wednesday (FR30YR=RR), and the UK's 30-year yield at 5.553%, after borrowing costs hit their highest level since 1998 earlier in the week.

The benchmark 10-year German yield was at 2.7051% (DE10YT=RR). German industrial orders unexpectedly fell in July, data on Friday showed.

Yields on 30-year Treasuries (US30YT=RR) were at 4.8533%, having touched their lowest in three weeks during Asian trading.

The U.S. dollar eased, with the dollar index down 0.2% at 98.023 , while the euro was up 0.3% at $1.1683 .

After months of negotiations, the U.S. signed a deal to impose lower auto tariffs on Japan. The dollar was down 0.2% against the yen, with the pair at 148.21 .

Oil prices were in their third day of declines. Brent crude futures fell 0.6% to $66.56 a barrel, while U.S. West Texas Intermediate crude eased 0.8% to $63.00.

The European Union's energy commissioner said the EU would welcome U.S. President Donald Trump's plan to stop buying Russian oil.

Gold was steady at $3,546.24, having hit a record peak of $3,578.50 on Wednesday.

data.title: Inmates on hunger strike at French high-security prison for drug traffickers
data.description:
Inmates at a French high-security prison for dangerous drug traffickers started a hunger strike this week to protest against their detention conditions, a Justice Ministry spokesperson said, confirming French media reports.

The strike at the Vendin-le-Vieil prison in the northern Pas-de-Calais region started on Monday. It was too early to say exactly how many inmates were part of the movement, he said.

"The prison is functioning, the inmates are restless, and that is precisely why they are attempting such an operation," the spokesperson said.

"They are unsettled as they find themselves in a prison that is sealed off from the outside world, where they can no longer manage their trafficking from inside the prison as they did before."

He said the Justice Ministry would not "give in" but did not elaborate on the inmates' demands.

French daily Le Figaro, citing a prison source, reported earlier this week that a few days after some prisoners flooded their cells with water, about 20 inmates in the first wing of the prison went on a hunger strike.

Eighty-eight convicted drug traffickers were transferred to the special wing of the prison in July as part of Justice Minister Gerald Darmanin's plans to toughen up prison conditions for some of France's most dangerous inmates.

Rubber Futures at Over 1-Month High
data.description:
Rubber futures continued to climb toward 175 US cents per kilogram, the highest level since late July, amid growing supply concerns from weather-related disruptions in key producing regions like Thailand, Vietnam and China.

Heavy seasonal rains in Southeast Asia have intensified in recent weeks due to more frequent tropical storms, a pattern increasingly associated with climate change.

However, upside momentum was restrained by declining oil prices and ongoing worries over sluggish global tyre demand and excess supply in China.

data.title: India stocks, rupee, swaps, call at close
data.description:
STOCKS:

The benchmark BSE Sensex was little changed at 80,710.76 and the broader NSE Nifty 50 index was flat at 24,741 after a volatile Friday, as investors booked gains in consumer stocks and a slide in information technology shares offset broader advances.

RUPEE:

The Indian rupee on Friday hit its lifetime low of 88.36 versus the U.S. dollar, pressured by worries over U.S. tariffs on India but likely central bank intervention helped the currency limit its losses, closing 0.1% lower at 88.2650.

GOVERNMENT BONDS:

The benchmark 10-year bond < IN063335G=CC> was quoted at 99.03 rupees, with the yield down 3 bps at 6.4651% as traders unwound short positions on easing fiscal concerns and an assurance from government officials that the budget gap target for the year will be met.

OVERNIGHT INDEX SWAPS:

The one-year overnight index swap rate (INR1YMIBROIS=CC) was down more than 2 basis points at 5.485%, while the five-year swap rate (INR5YMIBROIS=CC) dropped 3 bps to 5.715%.

CALL MONEY:

India's overnight call money rate (INROND=) was at 5% and the overnight TREPS rate (INTREPTOT=TCCL) was at 5.10%

data.title: BBVA Clears Last Hurdle to Open $17.4 Billion Bid to Sabadell Shareholders — Update
data.description:
By Elena Vardon

Spain's BBVA got the last outstanding regulatory approval for its $17.4 billion bid to take over smaller peer Banco de Sabadell and said it would open the offer to shareholders starting next week.

Banco Bilbao Vizcaya Argentaria--as the bank is formally known--went hostile in its pursuit of Sabadell in May last year with the aim of creating one of the biggest lenders in the eurozone after it encountered fierce opposition from the management of its smaller rival.

The bid now values Sabadell at around 14.9 billion euros, equivalent to $17.42 billion. This is up from the 12.2 billion euros the offer was valued at when it was first floated, as the bid is tied to the stock prices of both banks, which have fluctuated as the takeover battle dragged on for more than a year amid several regulatory reviews. The exchange ratio was also adjusted to account for dividend payments.

Spain's National Securities Market Commission on Friday said it authorized the transaction with the understanding that its terms complied with applicable regulations and the prospectus's contents were sufficient following recent amendments.

Shareholders in Sabadell will have from Monday until Oct. 7 to take up the offer, BBVA said.

Sabadell, which has rejected the offer, declined to comment until its board issues a formal opinion on the offer. It has 10 working days to do so starting on Monday.

The deal had already been approved by Spain's competition regulator, after BBVA put forward remedies to address antitrust concerns, as well as by the European Central Bank.

The Spanish government added more conditions when it approved the deal, including a ban on a legal merger of the two banks for at least three years--a period over which their entities and assets would have to be kept separate and operate independently.

Despite this setback, and Sabadell's sale of its U.K. unit TSB to Santander, BBVA decided to stick with the transaction, saying it still sees value despite the risks. BBVA expects 900 million euros in synergies, up from a previous 850 million euros estimate, though it anticipates these to be achieved one year later than initially planned, in 2029, due to Madrid's interference.

BBVA said the value of its offer would rise to 17.4 billion euros when adding dividends and buybacks Sabadell pledged to pay after the TSB sale.

"The union of two highly complementary banks at their best moment has an undeniable logic," BBVA Chair Carlos Torres Vila said on Friday. "Now is the time," he added.

BBVA, whose shares also trade in the U.S., separately said it was authorized by the Securities and Exchange Commission to lower the acceptance threshold of the tender offer to 30% from 50% of voting rights. The bank would then be required under Spanish law to launch a second all-cash tender offer for the remaining shares if it secures between 30% and 50% of voting rights.

Shares in BBVA traded 1% in European afternoon trading, while Sabadell's were up 0.4%.

Write to Elena Vardon at elena.vardon@wsj.com
